title: Apply: thermodynamics
description: Apply your understanding of thermodynamics in this set of free practice questions aligned to NGSS standards.

assessment_items.question_content:
On a cold day, Andre brings in a chair from outside and places it in a greenhouse. The glassed-in room of the greenhouse has been warmed by the sun. 

**What temperature changes will occur in this system?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The room’s air will get warmer because heat flows to it from the chair.
The room’s air will get cooler because heat flows from it to the chair.
The chair will get warmer because heat flows to it from the room’s air.
The chair will get cooler because heat flows from it to the room’s air.

assessment_items.question_content:
Mimi brings a cooler to a picnic at the beach. She places an unrefrigerated canned drink alongside a cold water bottle in the cooler.

**How will the temperatures of the objects in the system change?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The water bottle will remain at the same temperature because no heat flows to or away from it.
The water bottle will get warmer as heat flows to it.
The can will remain at the same temperature because no heat flows to or away from it.
The can will get cooler because heat flows away from it.

assessment_items.question_content:
Nikola brings in a blanket from his room and places it on a sofa in the living room. The air in the living room is slightly warmer than the blanket.

**Which temperature changes will occur in this system?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The room’s air will get warmer because heat flows to it from the blanket.
The room’s air will get cooler because heat flows from it to the blanket.
The blanket will get warmer because heat flows to it from the room’s air.
The blanket will get cooler because heat flows from it to the room’s air.

assessment_items.question_content:
You are trying to find the amount of heat transferred between two substances. In order to do this, you plan to place both substances in a thermally isolated container and wait until the system reaches thermal equilibrium. You need to decide what measurements you should take.

Remember that heat transfer is described by the equation $Q = mc \Delta T$ where $m$ is the mass, $c$ is the specific heat, and $\Delta T$ is the change in temperature. Assume you are able to look up the specific heat capacities of both substances.

**Which of the following sets of measurements would best allow you to determine the heat transfer between the two substances?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the initial masses and temperatures of the two substances and the final temperature of the system
the initial volumes and temperatures of the two substances and the final temperature of the system
the total masses of the two substances, the initial temperatures of the two substances, and the final temperature of the system

assessment_items.question_content:
You are trying to find the amount of heat transferred between two substances. In order to do this, you plan to place both substances in a thermally isolated container and wait until the system reaches thermal equilibrium. You need to decide what measurements you should take.

Remember that heat transfer is described by the equation $Q = mc \Delta T$ where $m$ is the mass, $c$ is the specific heat, and $\Delta T$ is the change in temperature. Assume you are able to look up the specific heat capacities of both substances.

**Which of the following sets of measurements would best allow you to determine the heat transfer between the two substances?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the total volume of the two substances and the final temperature of the system.
the initial masses of the two substances and the final temperature of the system
the initial mass and temperature of one of the substances and the final temperature of the system

assessment_items.question_content:
You are trying to find the amount of heat transferred between two substances. In order to do this, you plan to place both substances in a thermally isolated container and wait until the system reaches thermal equilibrium. You need to decide what measurements you should take.

Remember that heat transfer is described by the equation $Q = mc \Delta T$ where $m$ is the mass, $c$ is the specific heat, and $\Delta T$ is the change in temperature. Assume you are able to look up the specific heat capacities of both substances.

**Which of the following sets of measurements would best allow you to determine the heat transfer to substance A?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the initial masses and temperatures of the two substances and the final mass of the system
the initial masses and temperatures of the two substances and the final temperature of substance B
the total masses of the two substances, the initial temperatures of the two substances, and the final temperature of the system

assessment_items.question_content:
A power plant that uses steam to turn turbines produces $40.0 \degree \text{C}$ hot water. The manufacturer must cool this water before releasing it. So, the manufacturer mixes every $1.00 \, \text{kg}$ of its hot water with $3.00 \, \text{kg}$ of $20.0 \degree \text{C}$ water.

**Calculate the final temperature of the water.**  
*Round your answer to the nearest tenth.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\degree \text{C}$
hints:
To calculate the temperature change when two substances are mixed, we can use the fact that the energy lost from one substance is equal to the amount gained from the other substance. In some cases, we would set our change in energy equations for each substance, $Q = mC(T_f - T_i)$, equal to one another and solve for the final temperature.

However, in this case, we only have one substance which does not change phase, so we can use a simpler method: finding the weighted average of the temperature of the water.

To find the weighted average of the temperature, we can set up the following equation, multiplying the temperature of each amount of water by the mass of that water:

$T_f = \dfrac{m_hT_h+m_cT_c}{m_h+m_c}$
So, the final temperature of the water is $25.0 \degree \text{C}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Janelle gets hot coffee with a mass of $0.0400 \, \text{kg}$ from a pitcher at her company cafeteria. The coffee in the pitcher has a constant temperature of $80.0 \degree \text{C}$. By the time she drinks two-thirds of the coffee, it has cooled down to $56.0 \degree \text{C}$. She replaces the coffee she has drunk with hot coffee from the pitcher. 

**Calculate the final temperature of the replenished coffee.**  
*Round your answer to the nearest tenth.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\degree \text{C}$
hints:
To calculate the temperature change when two substances are mixed, we can use the fact that the energy lost from one substance is equal to the amount gained from the other substance. In some cases, we would set our change in energy equations for each substance, $Q = mC(T_f - T_i)$, equal to one another and solve for the final temperature.

However, in this case, we only have one substance which does not change phase, so we can use a simpler method: finding the weighted average of the temperature of the coffee.

To find the weighted average of the temperature, we can set up the following equation, multiplying the temperature of each amount of coffee by the mass of that coffee:

$T_f = \dfrac{m_hT_h+m_cT_c}{m_h+m_c}$
So, the final temperature of the replenished coffee is $72.0 \degree \text{C}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
A newly designed water heater maintains water at $60.0 \degree \text{C}$ in order to prevent the growth of microorganisms. However, this water temperature can scald a person, so the water heater is used with specially designed faucets that mix hot and cold water at the tap. The tap mixes $1.00 \, \text{kg}$ of hot water with a minimum of $0.0400 \, \text{kg}$ of $8.00 \degree \text{C}$ water.

**Calculate the maximum temperature of the water at the tap.**  
*Round your answer to the nearest tenth.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\degree \text{C}$
hints:
To calculate the temperature change when two substances are mixed, we can use the fact that the energy lost from one substance is equal to the amount gained from the other substance. In some cases, we would set our change in energy equations for each substance, $Q = mC(T_f - T_i)$, equal to one another and solve for the final temperature.

However, in this case, we only have one substance which does not change phase, so we can use a simpler method: finding the weighted average of the temperature of the water.

To find the weighted average of the temperature, we can set up the following equation, multiplying the temperature of each amount of water by the mass of that water:

$T_f = \dfrac{m_hT_h+m_cT_c}{m_h+m_c}$
So, the maximum temperature of the water is $58.0 \degree \text{C}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
In a metal processing plant, Tyrese is testing a new instrument that measures heat transfer and temperature. He cools a $100.0 \degree \text{C}$, $32.0 \, \text{g}$ piece of iron by placing it in $50.0 \, \text{g}$ of water at $50.0 \degree \text{C}$. As the iron cools, $1590 \, \text{J}$ of heat is transferred to the water. Assume no energy is lost from the system. Use $c = 4{,}190 \, \text{J/kg}\degree \text{C}$ for water.

**Calculate the final temperature of the water-iron control system.**  
*Write your answer rounding to the nearest tenth.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\degree \text{C}$
hints:
To find the system’s final temperature, we can start with the initial temperature of one of the system’s components. Then, we can add or subtract the temperature change that component would experience as the system reaches equilibrium. Since the water is gaining energy, our equation starting with water would be:

$T_{\text{W}_f} = T_{\text{W}_i} + \Delta T$

The equation for heat transfer is $Q = mc \Delta T$, where $Q$ is the heat transferred, $\Delta T$ is the change in temperature, $m$ is the mass, and $c$ is the specific heat capacity. We can rearrange this equation to solve for change in temperature:

$\begin{align}
Q &= mc \Delta T
\\\\
\Delta T &= \dfrac{Q}{mc}
\end{align}$

Next we can plug this into our equation for the final temperature of the water:

$T_{\text{W}_f} = T_{\text{W}_i} + \dfrac{Q}{m_\text{W} \cdot c_\text{W}}$
So, the final temperature of the water-iron system is about $57.6 \degree \text{C}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
On a hot day, the deck of a small ship reaches a temperature of $48.0 \degree \text{C}$. The crew pours $76.0 \, \text{kg}$ of $17.0 \degree \text{C}$ seawater on the deck to cool it down. During the cooling, $3{,}710{,}000 \, \text{J}$ are transferred to the seawater from the deck. Assume no energy or mass is lost from the system. Use $c = 3{,}930  \, \text{J/kg}\degree \text{C}$ for seawater.

**Calculate the final temperature of the seawater-deck system.**  
*Write your answer rounding to the nearest tenth.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\degree \text{C}$
hints:
To find the system’s final temperature, we can start with the initial temperature of one of the system’s components. Then, we can add or subtract the temperature change that component would experience as the system reaches equilibrium. Since the seawater is gaining energy, our equation starting with seawater would be:

$T_{\text{W}_f} = T_{\text{W}_i} + \Delta T$

The equation for heat transfer is $Q = mc \Delta T$, where $Q$ is the heat transferred, $\Delta T$ is the change in temperature, $m$ is the mass, and $c$ is the specific heat capacity. We can rearrange this equation to solve for change in temperature:

$\begin{align}
Q &= mc \Delta T
\\\\
\Delta T &= \dfrac{Q}{mc}
\end{align}$

Next we can plug this into our equation for the final temperature of the seawater:

$T_{\text{W}_f} = T_{\text{W}_i} + \dfrac{Q}{m_\text{W} \cdot c_\text{W}}$
So, the final temperature of the seawater-deck system is about $29.4 \degree \text{C}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Mimi wants to thaw out a frozen chicken with a temperature of $0.100 \degree \text{C}$. She places the chicken in $2.00 \, \text{kg}$ of water with a temperature of $33.0 \degree \text{C}$. As the water cools, $97{,}500 \, \text{J}$ of heat is transferred to the chicken. Assume no energy is lost from the system. Use $c = 4{,}190 \, \text{J/kg}\degree \text{C}$ for water.

**Calculate the final temperature of the water-chicken system.**  
*Write your answer rounding to the nearest tenth.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\degree \text{C}$
hints:
To find the system’s final temperature, we can start with the initial temperature of one of the system’s components. Then, we can add or subtract the temperature change that component would experience as the system reaches equilibrium. Since the water is losing energy, our equation starting with water would be:

$T_{\text{W}_f} = T_{\text{W}_i} - \Delta T$

The equation for heat transfer is $Q = mc \Delta T$, where $Q$ is the heat transferred, $\Delta T$ is the change in temperature, $m$ is the mass, and $c$ is the specific heat capacity. We can rearrange this equation to solve for change in temperature:

$\begin{align}
Q &= mc \Delta T
\\\\
\Delta T &= \dfrac{Q}{mc}
\end{align}$

Next we can plug this into our equation for the final temperature of the water:

$T_{\text{W}_f} = T_{\text{W}_i} - \dfrac{Q}{m_\text{W} \cdot c_\text{W}}$
So, the final temperature of the water-chicken system is about $21.4 \degree \text{C}$.
